## Tiny Language Model Guided Flow Q-learning for Optimal Task Scheduling in Fog Computing

Academic Editor: Frank Werner

Received: 9 December 2025

Revised: 7 January 2026

Accepted: 8 January 2026

Published: 10 January 2026

Copyright: ©2026 by the authors.

Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland.

This article is an open access article

distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons

Attribution (CC BY) license.

Bhargavi K 1, _ and Sajjan G. Shiva 2, _

- Department of CSE, Siddaganga Institute of Technology, Tumakuru 572103, Karnataka, India
- Department of CS, University of Memphis, Memphis, TN 38152, USA

* Correspondence: bhargavik@sit.ac.in (B.K.); sshiva@memphis.edu (S.G.S.)

## Abstract

Fog computing is one of the rapidly growing platforms with an exponentially increasing demand for real-time data processing. The fog computing market is expected to reach USD 8358 million by the year 2030 with a compound annual growth of 50%. The wide adaptation of fog computing by the industries worldwide is due to the advantages like reduced latency, high operational efficiency, and high-level data privacy. The highly distributed and heterogeneous nature of fog computing leads to significant challenges related to resource management, data security, task scheduling, data privacy, and interoperability. The task typically represents a job generated by the IoT device. The action indicates the way of executing the tasks whose decision is taken by the scheduler. Task scheduling is one of the prominent issues in fog computing which includes the process of effectively scheduling the tasks among fog devices to effectively utilize the resources and meet the Quality of Service (QoS) requirements of the applications. Improper task scheduling leads to increased execution time, overutilization of resources, data loss, and poor scalability. Hence there is a need to do proper task scheduling to make optimal task distribution decisions in a highly dynamic resource-constrained heterogeneous fog computing environment. Flow Q-learning (FQL) is a potential form of reinforcement learning algorithm which uses the flow matching policy for action distribution. It can handle complex forms of data and multimodal action distribution which make it suitable for the highly volatile fog computing environment. However, flow Q-learning struggles to achieve a proper trade-off between the expressive flow model and a reduction in the Q-function, as it relies on a one-step optimization policy that introduces bias into the estimated Q-function value. The Tiny Language Model (TLM) is a significantly smaller form of a Large Language Model (LLM) which is designed to operate over the device-constrained environment. It can provide fair and systematic guidance to disproportionally biased deep learning models. In this paper a novel TLM guided flow Q-learning framework is designed to address the task scheduling problem in fog computing. The neutrality and fine-tuning capability of the TLM is combined with the quick generable ability of the FQL algorithm. The framework is simulated using the Simcan2Fog simulator considering the dynamic nature of fog environment under finite and infinite resources. The performance is found to be good with respect to parameters like execution time, accuracy, response time, and latency. Further the results obtained are validated using the expected value analysis method which is found to be satisfactory.

Keywords: fog computing; tiny language model; flow Q-learning; service placement; optimal solution

## 1. Introduction

Fog computing is a popular distributed computing environment which brings computation and storage closer to the fog nodes. The proximity of the data towards the fog nodes enhances the processing efficiency by reducing the latency and response time. There is an exponential growth in fog computing as the need for real-time data processing at fog nodes is high. The fog computing market is expected to reach 8358 million dollars by the year 2030 with a compound annual growth of 50%. It offers greater scalability by seamlessly adding the fog nodes at the edge network, and the resources are provided on a demand basis. Because of the distributed nature of the fog system, single point failures are prevented, and the system is resilient to attacks. The improved coordination among the fog nodes leads to quick data analysis at edge devices, location awareness, and improved overall efficiency of the fog services [1, 2].

The dynamic nature typically represents time-specific varying characteristics of the fog computing system which affect the scheduling decisions. The tasks generated by the IoT devices vary continuously in unpredictable ways. The resource availability of fog nodes in terms of bandwidth, memory, and CPU utilization rate keeps fluctuating. Even the network conditions are not constant. The queue length keeps updating as the system state changes after every scheduling decision. The heterogeneity of the fog system represents unequal computation capability among the fog nodes. The energy, power, and latency across fog nodes also keep varying. Fog nodes are also spread across a wide geographic location, and every fog node cannot have a global and static view of the requirements. Hence it is not possible to formulate a single scheduling policy which is optimal for all heterogeneous task requests. Managing this uncertainty is challenging and demands the design and development of a fine-tuned machine learning model [3, 4]. The machine learning model is mainly required to handle the high-dimensional, time-varying, and partially observable nature of fog computing. The simple heuristic solution is not feasible for fog computing as it is dependent on fixed-decision logic, limited awareness of fog states, and the static assumption of network characteristics. The machine learning model should understand system behavior and mine nonlinear relationships among fog nodes. The generic form of the machine learning algorithm suffers from poor exploration, slow convergence, and unstable learning over the large state space environment. The language model fine-tuned the machine learning algorithm by injecting the prior knowledge, including a guide during exploration, and accelerating the convergence process [5, 6]. The fine-tuned machine learning models are considered as promising frameworks to address the performance issues in the fog environment like resource management, task scheduling, service placement, resource provisioning, and many more.

Out of all performance issues, task scheduling is considered as one of the most difficult tasks in an uncertain fog environment because of various factors like the arrival rate of tasks, fluctuating resource demands, and unpredictable network conditions. As the tasks are generated continuously, the workload demands fluctuate dynamically. The resource requirements of tasks generated by the heterogeneous IoT devices keeps fluctuating. The network congestion and failure of fog devices can cause significant damage to the performance of the fog system [5, 6]. As a result, the chances of formulating the improper task scheduling decisions are high. Improper task scheduling policies are the policies that operate randomly by ignoring the current state of operation. This causes long-term side effects through violation of Service Level Agreements (SLAs) and inefficient usage of resources. Various kinds of improper task scheduling happen that include random assignment, greedy assignment, threshold-based assignment, and round robin assignment. In random assignment, the task is randomly assigned to the computing fog node without taking into consideration current load and latency sensitivity of the tasks. In greedy assignment, all incoming tasks are assigned to the nearest fog node intentionally even though that node becomes overloaded and other nodes are underloaded. In threshold-based assignments, a fixed threshold value is predetermined based on which decisions are made despite the fluctuations in the network and task requirements. In round robin assignment, the tasks are distributed among the fog nodes sequentially with fixed time slots for processing, in which the heterogeneous nature of the tasks and resources are completely neglected. Hence there is a need to develop a task scheduler which can handle uncertainties and formulate high quality task scheduling policies [7].

In literature, several classical heuristic algorithms and machine learning algorithms are developed for task scheduling in fog environments. Heuristic algorithms are dependent on the fixed assumptions which fail to operate in a real-time fog computing environment. The task's arrival pattern is usually bursty and resources availability changes continuously. The predetermined rules do not learn from experience and lack online adaptation. They perform manual fine tuning of parameters and assume all tasks have identical QoS requirements. As the complexity of the rules increases there is a communication overhead and parameter fine tuning becomes infeasible. The partial observability and uncertain execution pattern of the fog environment weakens the scheduler performance. Hence, the learning base approaches the need to be designed to learn through experience and make long-term decisions through adaptability. Machine learning models directly capture nonlinear interaction between tasks, fog nodes, and network. Through trial and feedback mechanisms the machine learning algorithm becomes tolerant to uncertainty and gains the ability to generalize from experience [8]

Flow Q-learning (FQL) is one of the promising offline forms of reinforcement learning techniques which models complex action distributions using flow matching policy. The existing actor critic framework is updated using flow matching policy and does not require any form of sampling strategy. The flow policies are basic, and maximum value is reached using recursive backpropagation. The core idea is to use a one-step policy along with the flow policy with behavioral cloning to maximize the Q-value. The performance of the FQL is better than the Gaussian policies as it uses weighted regression, recursive backpropagation, and rejection sampling. However, FQL performance suffers because of overestimation of action values and is very sensitive to hyperparameter values. It also struggles to achieve a proper trade-off between exploration and exploitation. The size of the Q table grows exponentially and takes a longer time to converge to an optimal solution due to slow updates of Q-values. It also struggles with poor reward shaping and heuristics that are not available for sampling the state and action pairs [9, 10]. It becomes difficult for the FQL algorithm to learn from scratch about complex dynamics of the fog computing environment, so Tiny Language Models (TLMs) provide guidance to FQL in a structured manner which accelerates the speed to learning and stabilizes it quickly.

TLMs are potential lightweight alternatives to Large Language Models (LLMs). The main motivation to select a TLM is that it can operate in a resource-constrained environment. It also offers low inference overhead and allows for near-edge deployment. It is made up of fewer parameters which are properly fine-tuned and can deliver high-level performance using strategies such as byte-level tokenization, pooling, and weight training. The TLMs are better compared to generic Large Language Models, deep neural networks, heuristic models, and centralized cloud approaches. The generic language models include Large Language Model Meta AI (LLaMA), Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT), and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT). The deep neural networks include Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and Q-learning. Heuristic approaches include the Genetic Algorithm (GA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). Centralized cloud approaches include deep Q network (DQN), Software-Defined Network (SDN), and Kubernetes . The heuristics of TLM act as source of guidance for the flow Q-learning algorithm. The sampling efficiency of the FQL is enhanced at every step of training, which in turn provides the desired policies in subsequent iteration. It also extends support for the online and offline corrections of heuristics which prevents biased performance in a dynamic fog environment. The heuristics of TLM speed up the learning capability of the FQL agent and recover seamlessly from the incorrect guidance [11, 12].

In this paper a novel TLM guided FQL framework is designed for the fog computing environment. The FQL enriched with the heuristics of the TLM can formulate optimal task scheduling policies even from imperfect data by considering the suggestions from generative artificial intelligence models. Immediate shaping of Q-values is carried out according to the heuristics of TLM which enhances the reward shaping mechanism [13]. The unnecessary explorations over the state space are prevented through pre-sampling and post-sampling of the state space under the guidelines of the TLM [14, 15].

The objectives of the paper are as follows:

- Abrief introduction to fog computing and discussing the factors causing uncertainties which influence the task scheduler performance.
- Enriching the traditional FQL framework with the heuristics of the TLM to formulate task scheduling policies which are free from bias within a few epochs of training.
- Mathematical representation of the fog computing system model with the definitions for the performance metrics considered for evaluation purpose.
- Performing a literature survey over the recent existing works and summarizing the research gaps identified.
- Design of a novel TLM guided FQL framework to perform task scheduling.
- Expected value analysis of the TLM guided FQL framework considering finite and infinite fog computing scenarios.
- Simulation of the TLM guided FQL framework using Simcan2Fog simulator considering real-time and synthetic workloads.

The remaining sections of the paper are organized as follows: Section 2 discusses the related work, Section 3 gives the system model for fog computing, Section 4 discusses the proposed TLM guided flow Q framework, Section 5 performs expected value analysis of the proposed framework, Section 6 discusses the result and discussion, and finally Section 7 ends with the conclusions.

## 2. Related Work

In this section some of the potential recent papers on task scheduling for fog computing are discussed and their limitations are identified.

Choppara et al. presents an adaptive form of the task scheduling framework for fog computing by combining the federated deep Q network with the K-means clustering algorithm [16]. Each of the fog nodes in the fog environment is made capable enough for training the local deep Q network model. The experience of each of the fog nodes will be merged to formulate a global model without the need to share any sensitive information. The federated nature of learning provides a self-optimizing solution which evenly spreads the learning among all fog nodes. The priorities are assigned for the incoming tasks considering the two parameters of execution time and deadline for completion using the K-means clustering algorithm. Care is always taken such that higher priority tasks are executed by minimizing the rejection rate and Service Level Agreement violations. The model continuously upgrades itself and adapts to a dynamically changing environment through iterative learning. The localized form of learning incorporates constant learning among local deep Q-learning agents which constantly update the task scheduling policies considering both task and environment characteristics. The experimental evaluation over the larger dataset ensures that makespan time is reduced and throughput is enhanced. Since distributed task scheduling is performed, the framework acts as a flexible solution for latency-sensitive applications. For a larger network like fog, decentralization themed task scheduling enhances the system scalability and reduces the communication overhead but demands for high synchronization. Prioritized experience replay is performed in which higher priorities enable the model to focus over the most informative experience. However, the framework demands frequent updates from the sender and receiver models which makes it expensive. The deeply nested Q network often causes instability of the network due to frequent updating of the Q-value and higher sampling efficiency over the large state space environment.

Choppara et al. discuss a flexible task scheduling framework based on the deep deterministic form of policy gradient (DDPG) for fog computing [17]. The DDPG is dynamically adaptable for continuous and large state space environments in which the task scheduling policies adapt themselves according to real-time demands of the tasks in terms of resources. The DDPG basically follows the actor critic learning model in which the actor learns the policy and the critic evaluates the action taken using the Q-function value. The experimental results reveal that satisfactory performance is achieved by reducing the makespan time and increasing the fault tolerance. The data processing operation is localized which reduces the bandwidth requirement and enhances the efficiency of data management across the fog nodes. The priority score is calculated for each of the tasks considering the key parameters like resource requirement, duration of execution, task deadline, and dependency for the tasks. Critical applications and delay-sensitive applications are executed first based on the priority score. After prioritization, the tasks are classified into three categories which include sequential tasks, parallel tasks, and dependency-oriented tasks. For sequential tasks, an execution order will be defined which is well-suited for data pipeline applications. The parallel tasks are executed on a certain set of virtual machines which are mounted over the fog nodes. For dependency-based tasks, logical consistency is ensured in the workflow which demands to satisfy the specific requirements prior to execution. However, the framework is subjected to a divergence problem due to high variance encountered in the estimation of the Q-value. Also, a tendency to end up in local optima is high due to high sensitivity towards hyperparameters and sparse reward in the deterministic fog computing environment.

Sudheer et al. discuss a methodology for task scheduling in fog computing by combining priority awareness approach with the simulated annealing mechanism [18]. The simulated annealing algorithm begins with the initialization of the state space with sub optimal solutions. Later the optimality of the solution will be improved by decreasing the value of the temperature variable. The random task plan is assigned for each of the fog nodes at first. Afterwards a small tweak in the task plan is performed by considering the parameters like energy consumption, cost of execution, completion time of task, and priority assigned for the tasks. This leads to the generation of neighbors and a controlled exploration of the state space is performed. The small tweak performed affects only two workloads: the old node which was executing the workload and the new node to which the workload was transferred. Initially the temperature is set to be high, but the algorithm slowly reduces the temperature value through the hierarchy to converge with the best potential solution in a fine-grained approach. The scheduling policies are improved gradually by computing the cost for each of the small changes in an iterative manner. During the annealing phase, higher weightage is given for exploring the new solutions instead of revisiting the existing solutions. The movement is carried out in an uphill manner by accepting all the worst forms of solutions which allow the exploration of state space in a wider manner. The proper trade-off is achieved between exploration and exploitation through tough optimization process. During the cooling phase, the gradual cooling of temperature is achieved to arrive at an ideal solution while keeping the search area open wide enough for traversal. Finally, algorithms stop when the temperature falls below the threshold or the k number of iterations are completed. The performance results show that the high priority tasks are processed early using a priority aware penalty function. The solution space is approached in a very strict and controlled manner this prevents large shifts leading to a local optimal solution. However, the algorithm is completely stochastic in nature which leads to different kinds of outcome at every run. The cooling process is slow and requires more epochs of training compared to the traditional spring embedders technique.

Asad et al. presents a novel task scheduling framework for a cloud and fog environment using the Harris Hawk Optimization (HHO) technique [19]. Task scheduling is an important issue as it is difficult to find appropriate resources for the execution of the tasks. Scheduling the client task to fog nodes means tasks can be executed with less delay but it deals with more energy consumption. Meanwhile scheduling the tasks onto a cloud server consumes less energy but more delay is incurred. Hence it is essential for the task scheduling algorithm to consider all the factors including energy and delay before arriving at scheduling decisions. The HHO technique mimics the cooperative hunting strategy of the Harris Hawk. It mainly works in two phases: exploration and exploitation. During the exploration phase the location of prey is located by the Hawk. Similarly, during the exploitation phase, all possible attack strategies are executed over the prey. At first the arrival rate of tasks and energy consumed for escaping of prey are initialized. The task is assigned to fog nodes based on the factors like the Quality of Service (QoS) requirements of tasks and location of the data source. The diversity in the available solution is determined using inverse mutation operation. The best solution out of all available solutions is stored in the repository as the non-dominated solution. Through epochs of training the new solution replaces the old solution based on the value of crowding distance. The soft besiege phase represents the start of the attacking strategy in which the energy level of the prey is depleted using the encircling mechanism. Similarly, the hard besiege phase performs encircling of the prey in a zigzag manner. The deep dive operation of the Hawks is performed using the Levy flight procedure. Whenever the prey becomes fatigued an effective attack will be launched to execute the surprise pounce. The process is repeated until termination is reached and the best solution available at the search agent is considered as the optimal scheduling policy. The experimental results indicate that there is a significant reduction in energy consumption and delay. Guaranteed performance is achieved by achieving proper balance between intensification and diversification phases. However, random parameters are involved in the exploration and exploitation phases which cause negative effects over the convergence speed and lead to higher computational cost.

Seyed et al. perform the scheduling of a bunch of tasks in fog computing using the discrete cuckoo search algorithm [20]. First the incoming client applications are broken down into many independent tasks. A centralized broker node is maintained which is responsible for maintaining the capacity of fog nodes along with their failure rates. The broker node is responsible for performing load balancing and allocating the task onto the most reliable fog node. The myriad permutation policy is followed by delivery of different varieties of solutions to the incoming client requests. The typical cuckoo search algorithm is converted into hybrid form by incorporating two procedures related to sorting and crowding distance computation. Numerous local search operators are used to perform exhaustive search over the solution space which consists of the worst solution and best solution. The discrete search space of the fog is explored towards the efficient form of quest permutations. The diverse novel exploration operation is invoked randomly using the Levy flight search mechanism. The workflow is typically represented as a directed acyclic graph; the exploration and exploitation operators are executed during the encoding stage. The topological ordering of the tasks is determined to preserve the constraints among the tasks. The main aim of the optimization is to generate new capable solutions over an iteration of training. If the new solution generated is better than the previous solution, then the new one is considered as the dominant solution. Here multiple objectives are taken into consideration while delivering the solution. The solutions are divided into two sets that are an inferior set of solutions and superior set of solutions using Pareto ranking values. The superior set of solutions focuses on performing multiple objectives optimization. Whereas the inferior set of solutions focuses on finding the single objective optimization. The set of solutions which do not compete are assigned to first rank and known as non-dominated solutions. The first rank solutions are removed from the current solutions, and the procedure is repeated for the remaining solutions. Likewise, second and third ranking solutions are represented. All solutions generated are sorted based on the number of objectives and crowding distance value. The solution with higher crowding distance value is considered as beneficial because it exhibits a high rate of diversity. The experimental results showcase that significant improvement is observed with respect to reliability and makespan time in all varying scenarios of workload conditions. However, here the scheduler is not aware of the user's Quality of Service requirements, which increases the monetary cost of operation and the scheduler fails to achieve the desired QoS.

Seohang et al. discussed the basic working of flow Q-learning algorithm [21]. It projects the flow matching policy to represent the complex action distribution in a large state space environment. The flow model is based on the stochastic differential equation which allows for faster training and converges with better quality solutions. Three important benefits offered by flow Q-learning are as follows: It uses a reparametrized policy gradient which perform effective exploration of state space and prevents the unstable and expensive backpropagation mechanism. Second, the one-step policy is generated as the output which overcomes the inherent limitations of iterative flow generation process. Third, the implementation is easy and fine tuning is performed at optimal pace. It is composed of only one major hyperparameter and does not demand fine tuning in a noisy environment. However, flow Q-learning suffers from training instability and poor generalization capability under dynamic scenario. It consists of many sensitive parameters which need to be fine-tuned and exhibits poor scalability due to the composition of a large input space. To overcome exploration inefficiency and solve the credit assignment problem, poor guidance is required.

Fuxue et al. discuss the need for LLM guidance to the traditional reinforcement learning algorithm [22]. The reinforcement learning algorithm cannot perform high-level planning and low-level task execution simultaneously. The LLM is responsible for decomposing larger tasks into a sequence of manageable subtasks in an interactive state space heterogeneous environment. During training even the intermediate form of subgoals are associated with the rewards and fine-grained feedback is provided to accelerate the convergence rate and perform structured exploration of the large state space environment. The chain of thought strategy is incorporated which allows the LLM to quickly update to changing dynamics of the environment state space. The exploration inefficiency in sparse reward setting is overcome by conditioning the subgoals and reward functions. The LLM guided Q-learning operates using generic prompts which prevent frequent retraining of the model. The reward is scaled as per the completion of subgoals, and more rewards are obtained through faster scaling. However, LLM-enabled reinforcement learning algorithms cannot be deployed for the fog environment, as LLMs are composed of billions of parame- ters and demand a large amount of memory. The output of LLM is often hallucinated and even invalid which makes it incompatible with the larger scale edge deployments scenarios. To summarize, most of the existing works exhibit the following drawbacks:

- The deep neural network is often stuck in learning instability over a large state space environment which leads to learning instability and converges with non-desirable policies [16].
- The deterministic policy gradients generated by the Q-learning agent are extremely sensitive towards the selection of hyperparameters. They also suffer from sparse reward problems [17].
- Metaheuristic algorithms are extremely costly over the distributed form of a heterogeneous environment. Quick scheduling decisions are taken using heuristic values which leads to errors in task scheduling decisions [19, 20].
- The rule-based algorithms usually fail to capture the uncertainty of the devices, workload, and network condition. Converges to suboptimal solutions due to improper balance between the exploration and exploitation activities [3].
- Supervised and unsupervised machine learning algorithms suffer from poor search progress and weaker efficiency. They are often subjected to overfitting problems and suffer from generalization issues due to high variance and learning instability [5, 6].
- Conventional flow Q-learning algorithms suffer from credit assignment problems and poor exploration capability [21].
- Traditional LLM guided reinforcement learning is subjected to the sparse reward problem and struggles to perform multistage scheduling in long horizons. Often ends up with invalid outputs and inefficient utilization of resources [22].

## 3. System Model

Thefogcomputingsystemmodeliscomposedofasetoftasks T = { T 1 , T 2, T 3, T 4 , . . . , Tm } and a set of fog nodes FN = { FN 1 , FN 2, FN 3, FN 4 , . . . , FNn } . Each fog node is made up of a collection of virtual machines VM = { VM 1 , VM 2, VM 3, VM 4 , . . . , VMk } . Each of the tasks is described using five attributes: task duration TD , resource requirements RR , deadline proximity DP , dependency count DC , and impact factor IF , i.e., Ti = < TD , RR , DP , DC , IF > . The TD is the estimated execution time of the tasks on any fog node. The RR is the computational resource requirement of the task to be executed. The DP indicates the deadline sensitivity of the task. The DC represents the number of tasks that are dependent on the execution of the current task. The impact factor IF is the composite measure of the severity of the poor scheduling decisions. For example: For healthcare application a sample attributes for a particular task includes Ti = < TD = 10 ms , RR = 200 CPUs , DP = Less , DC = 3 Tasks , IF = High > . For a small execution time, the IF value must be lesser for critical applications like healthcare and military in which improper scheduling results in severe consequences and ends up in poor Quality of Service (QoS). Similarly, each of the virtual machines represents the current workload C ( WL ) and capacity of the CPU, CP ( CPU ) , for all virtual machines in the fog node, i.e., VMi = < C ( WL ) , CP ( CPU ) > . The task scheduling problem is typically represented as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), MDP ( Ti ) = < S , A , P , R , γ > , where S is the state space, A is the action space, P is the transition probability, R is the reward function, and γ is the discount factor which achieves balance between the immediate reward and future reward. The description for the attributes of MDP are shown in Table 1.

The incoming tasks are arranged in a queue; the arrival and departure rate of the tasks are managed by a task scheduler. During FQL the training of flow policy is performed using a one-step policy for arbitrarily complex data distributed across the fog domain. Direct guiding of iterative flow is not performed instead the value of the one-step policy is maximized through distilling the flow model. As a result, the unstable recursive backpropagation is prevented which eliminates the need for costly action generation in the iterative mode. All the last optimal task scheduling policies are generated Π = < Π 1 , Π 2, Π 3, . . . , Π p > .

The mathematical definition for the evaluation metrics (EM) considered for evaluation of the framework is defined as follows. The reason behind the selection of these evaluation metrics is that they are the benchmark metrics followed worldwide for fog deployment. They basically represent the main objectives and constraints of a fog computing environment. In which the task requirements are dynamic, resources are less, but still there is a need to provide best Quality of Service (QoS). Cost metrics represent the economic efficiency of the task scheduler. Energy consumption metric is a critical evaluation metric because the scheduler needs to operate in a resource-constrained environment. SLA violation rate is important for delay-sensitive applications to meet the deadline and latency constraints. Scalability indicates the improved performance of the scheduler with the increasing number of tasks and fog nodes. Reliability is the ability of the scheduler to function even under fog nodes failure and network disruptions [23].

## 4. Proposed Work

The high-level architecture of TLM guided FQL framework is shown in Figure 1. The architecture is composed of two main components that are the Tiny Language Model (TLM) and flow Q-learning (FQL). TLM is a mini version of the Large Language Model (LLM) which has few parameters and can perform with lesser computational resources. The training of TLM is performed by a pre-trained model through knowledge distillation. Pruning is performed to remove unnecessary parts of the model and model weights are updated through quantization. Deploying TLM on edge devices allows ease of interaction among edge devices, analysis, and localization of the data. It begins with the preparation of its own data through model pretraining. The model is built as an open GPT-2 basic model whose parameters vary from 30 M to 124 M. Fine tuning of the data is performed through downstream embedding of the sensed tasks. The trained model is converted into an optimized model to provide guidance to the FQL.

Figure 1. The proposed high-level architecture of the TLM guided FQL framework.

The flowchart representation of the TLM guided FQL is given in Figure 2. The FQL considers the heuristics of TLM to generate flow policies through precise modeling of the complex action distributions in the fog domain. Instead of performing random initialization of the parameters of the Q-learning agent, the TLM are prompted to generate state, action, and Q-values based on the high-level description of the tasks and fog nodes. This provides a promising starting point for the agent and only significant heuristics are added to the Q-value update function which leads to execution of the best action at any given state of operation. The diffusion policies are trained to maximize the learned value function of the Q-learning agent. behavior cloning is a form of supervised learning which trains the Q-learning agent by mimicking the expert demonstrations. The sampling efficiency is improved through behavioral cloning, and it is also less subjected to compounding errors. Traditional Q-learning algorithms are trained using multi-step policies which suffer from performance degradation as it is highly sensitive towards the number of steps chosen. This problem is overcome here using a one-step policy which enables direct one-step action prediction from the state through the flow matching mechanism. Direct mapping is performed from state to action, and the necessity of a separate iterative process is eliminated by the learning velocity field which maps noise to action. The performance of the FQL is higher compared to the Gaussian model and diffusion model as it can easily learn multimodal action distributions in a complex environment. Even the poor heuristics of the TLM are handled effectively by the Q-learning agent through environment feedback and it is ensured that the task scheduling policies are unbiased and optimal.

Figure 2. Flowchart representation of TLM guided FQL framework.

Algorithm 1 provides detailed discussion on the workings of TLM guided FQL framework. The fog computing system sate space is defined by five attributes, i.e., FC \_ SS = { RFN , QFN , EFN , LFN , TFN } , RFN is the resources at the fog nodes, QFN is the queue length at each of the fog node, EFN is the energy of the fog node, LFN is the network latency, and TFN is the attributes of the tasks. The concrete scheduling action is taken for each of the tasks, ai = { Execute , Of f load to cloud , Delay / reject } , by considering the heuristic scores of the actions generated by TLM. The input for TLM includes the characteristics of tasks, fog nodes, and the network condition. The state to encoder converts the numerical input into textual tokes. For example, if CPU utilization is greater than 80 percent it is considered as high load. TLM outputs a small set of action preference tokens. The deterministic action mapping subroutine is responsible for converting the tokens of TLN into heuristic scores. The heuristic score ranges between -0.5 and +1.0.

The FQL is mainly composed of three components, i.e., critic Q ∅ ( s , a ) , behavior cloning flow policy µθ ( s , z ) , and one-step policy µ w ( s , z ) . The critic basically provides learning signal via a gradient target for the flow policy generated by the agent. Bellman consistency is ensured by the critic by precisely estimating the future retunes. The behavior cloning flow policy trains the flow policy to mimic the action of the dataset and it becomes the starting point of the agent. It also teaches the flow model to map noise to action. After performing behavior cloning the Q-learning agent improves itself beyond the initialized dataset. The one-step policy takes the action immediately from a state without the need for any iterations. However, multiple steps of transformation are executed within to select a particular action as the optimal one. Using one-step policy the TLM_FQL parses through the tasks set by considering only the present Q state. This makes it greedy and leads to immediate scheduling decisions with multi-step planning.

## 6. Results and Discussion

The proposed TLM_FQL framework is compared with three of the recent existing works, i.e., DDPG [17], DCS [20], HHO [19], and FQL [21]. The task scheduling environment is composed of two layers: the IoT devices layer and fog layer. The IoT devices layer is responsible for generating the tasks which follow a stochastic arrival pattern. Similarly, the fog layer is composed of multiple fog nodes with varying processing capacity which process the tasks which are latency insensitive. If sufficient resources are not available, then they will be processed at the cloud layer with an unlimited number of resources. The TLM_FQL framework interacts with the fog environment based on the current state of operation. The current state is determined by considering the current dynamics of tasks, resources, and network conditions. The local task scheduling actions are chosen by the FQL considering the feedback of the TLM. The fog environment updates its state after the action and provides feedback over the evaluation metrics.

For simulation purposes Simcan2Fog simulator is used which is a recent extension of the fog computing simulator [26, 27]. It incorporates the real-time behavior of the network, hardware modeling, and cloud orchestration. It allows precise representation of network behavior considering topology management, obstacles detection, transport protocol, and traffic control mechanism. The Simcan2Fog simulator is an extension of the Simcan2Cloud simulator which provides higher end simulation features like virtualization, data center operations, queuing mechanism, and cost evaluations. ## 6.1. Small Scenario

The small scenario is localized and has a limited time frame which operates with a few tasks, sensors, actuators, controllers, fog nodes, and data centers.

## EM1: Cost (CO(TLM_FQL):

A graph of the number of fog nodes versus cost in the small scenario is shown in Figure 3. It is observed from the graph that the cost of TLM_FQL remained to be consistently less with the increase in the number of fog nodes, because its ability to handle complex and multimodal action space is well developed which yields superior performance. The cost of FQL kept increasing with the increase in the number of fog nodes as the Q-value estimation needed to be performed at every stage of training. The cost of DDPG is found to be moderate with the increase in the number of fog nodes, because it can handle the continuous state space environment, but it is sensitive towards hyperparameter initialization. The cost of DCS and HHO are found to be very high with the increase in the number of fog nodes, because of poor state space exploration and using random parameters during exploration stage which become trapped in local minima.

Figure 3. Number of fog nodes versus cost in small scenario.

## EM2: Energy consumption (EC(TLM_FQL):

Agraph of the number of tasks versus energy consumption in the small scenario is shown in Figure 4. It is observed from the graph that the energy consumption of TLM_FQL remained consistently low with the increase in the number of incoming tasks, as it can adapt effectively to changing dynamics of the network without the need for complex fine tuning of noise. The energy consumption of FQL is very high over the number of tasks because it becomes trapped in double instability and the error in the Q-value estimation propagates through the transmission of the flow policy. The energy consumption of DDPG is found to be moderate over the increase in the number of tasks, as it is subjected towards overestimation of the Q-values leading to suboptimal policies. The energy consumption of DCS and HHO are found to be very high with the increase in the number of tasks, as it demands frequent modification of core operators and poor prioritization of tasks, resulting in conflicting goals.

Figure 4. Number of tasks versus energy consumption in small scenario.

## EM3: Service Level Agreement (SLA) violation rate (SLAVR(TLM_FQL)):

A graph of the number of tasks versus SLA violation rate in the small scenario is shown in Figure 5. The SLA violation rate of TLM_FQL remained low with the increase in the number of tasks, because it can be directly fine-tuned for both offline and online rollouts. The SLA violation rate is high for FQL as learning signals weaken due to the diffusion of temporal difference error across the flow signals. The SLA violation of DDPG and DCS kept increasing with the increase in the number of tasks, because the policies are deterministic which leads to poor sampling efficiency and suboptimal solutions. The SLA violation of HHO is found to be moderate over the increase in the number of tasks due to its inability to perform a global search and inconsistency in the behavior at the individual and group level of operation.

Figure 5. Number of tasks versus SLA violation rate in small scenario.

## EM4: Scalability (SC(TLM_FQL)):

A graph of the number of tasks versus scale up in the small scenario is shown in Figure 6. It is observed from the graph that the TLM_FQL scales up easily with the increase in the number of tasks, as its scaling capacity is well developed since it used flow matching for training and avoids the accumulation of bias over the horizon. The scale up capability of FQL is less as the flow updated and is misguided due to the interference of errors in the Qfunction at the early stage of learning. The scale up capability of the DDPG and HHO are found to be poor with the increase in the number of tasks, because of unstable updates of Q-values leading to potential divergence towards function approximation. The scale up capability of DCS is found to be moderate with the increase in the number of tasks, as it becomes trapped in local minima and exhibits premature convergence without effectively exploring the state space.

Figure 6. Number of tasks versus scale up in small scenario.

## EM5: Reliability (RE(TLM_FQL)):

Agraph of the time versus failure rate in the small scenario is shown in Figure 7. It is observed from the graph that the failure rate of TLM_FQL is consistently less over time, as it effectively handles the training instability by using the one-step policy and policy decoupling strategy. The failure rate of FQL is high over the time as the optimal exploration of the state space is not guaranteed due to the involvement of latent space variables during exploration. The failure rate of DDPG and DCS are very high over time, as they find it difficult to adapt to discrete space from continuous state space environment. The failure rate of HHO is moderate over time. Due to the application of diversified hunting strategies, the technique is capable enough of assuring the optimal solution despite the changes in the fog nodes operating conditions.

Figure 7. Time versus failure rate in small scenario.

## 6.2. Big Scenario

The big scenario is expansive and spans a larger time which operates with a big number of tasks, sensors, actuators, controllers, fog nodes, and data centers.

## EM1: Cost (CO(TLM_FQL)):

Agraph of the number of fog nodes versus cost in the big scenario is shown in Figure 8. It is observed from the graph that the cost of TLM_FQL is consistently less with the increase in the number of fog nodes, because the one-step action process is faster and avoids the need for high computational expenses for sampling and backpropagation. The cost of FQL is high towards the increasing number of fog nodes as the Q-learning formulate outdated policies due to a slow understanding of fog nodes mobility and frequent failure of fog nodes. The cost of DDPG is found to be moderate with the increase in the number of fog nodes. Higher computation cost is incurred because it demands more memory and resources during forward passes of evaluation. The cost of DCS and HHO are found to be very high with the increase in the number of fog nodes, as they do long-time calculations and demand a significant number of resources to complete the execution.

Figure 8. Number of fog nodes versus cost in big scenario.

## EM2: Energy consumption (EC(TLM_FQL):

A graph of the number of tasks versus energy consumption in the big scenario is shown in Figure 9. It is observed from the graph that the energy consumption of TLM_FQL is consistently low with an increase in the number of tasks, as it prevents repeated recursive backpropagation through a multi-step sampling process. The energy consumption of FQL is high over the increase in the number of tasks as the flow transformations demand a large replay buffer for action storage and require diverse state space coverage to generalize the policies. The energy consumption of DDPG and HHO are found to be more with the increase in the number of tasks, as they suffer from an unstable optimization effect and are easily trapped into stagnation. The energy consumption of DCS is moderate with the increase in the number of tasks, because it has high computational complexity and struggles to adapt to a discrete problem state space environment.

Figure 9. Number of tasks versus energy consumption in big scenario.

## EM3: Service Level Agreement (SLA) violation rate (SLAVR(TLM_FQL)):

Agraph of the number of tasks versus SLA violation rate in the big scenario is shown in Figure 10. It is observed from the graph that the SLA violation rate of TLM_FQL is consistently less with an increase in the number of tasks. It is capable enough of achieving faster inference through ordinary deterministic differential equations. The SLA violation rate of FQL is high due to the generation of biased policies because of poor exploration of the state space environment. The SLA violation rate of DDPG is found to be moderate with the increase in the number of tasks, as it struggles to learn whenever subjected to sparse rewards. The SLA violation rate of DCS and HHO are found to be very high with the increase in the number of tasks. The initialization phase is in a randomized order which limits the diversity of solutions and results in local optima solutions.

Figure 10. Number of tasks versus SLA violation rate in big scenario.

## EM4: Scalability (SC(TLM_FQL)):

Agraph of the number of tasks versus scale up in the big scenario is shown in Figure 11. It is observed from the graph that the scalability of TLM_FQL is found to be very high with the increase in the number of tasks, as it avoids the need for backpropagation through multiple steps of the generation process. The scalability of FQL is less due to poor tuning of sensitive parameters like the discount factor, number of flow steps, and target updating rate. The scalability of DCS and HHO are found to be moderate with the increase in the number of tasks, as they are computationally expensive and demand complex conversion between continuous solutions and discrete solutions. The scalability of DDPG is found to be very low with the increase in the number of tasks. The inaccurate feedback leads to frequent reinforcement of suboptimal actions.

Figure 11. Number of tasks versus scale up in big scenario.

## EM5: Reliability (RE(TLM_FQL)):

A graph of time versus failure rate in the big scenario is shown in Figure 12. It is observed from the graph that the failure rate of TLM_FQL remained consistently less with respect to time. The flow policy is trained using the behavior cloning mechanism and achieves the maximum value function using the one-step policy. The failure rate of FQL is high as the constraints for task scheduling is handled only through penalty rewards and demands frequent model retraining with the increase in the heterogeneity of the fog nodes and tasks. The failure rate of DDPG and DCS are found to be moderate over time due to the poor balance between exploration and exploitation, with tendency towards premature convergence being high. The failure rate of HHO is found to be very high over time due to poor search ability and a chaotic optimization strategy causing negative impact over the exploration capability of the state space which destabilizes the functioning of the algorithm.

Figure 12. Time versus failure rate in big scenario.

## 7. Conclusions

The paper presents a novel Tiny Language Model-inspired flow Q-learning framework for task scheduling in fog computing. The heuristics of TLM are provided as guidance for Q-learning to perform flow models and behavior cloning of policies through one-step optimization. The structure priors of TLM reduce the tendency of overestimation of the Q-value and make the bootstrapping process smoother. Generalization of the behavior cloning is easy through demonstrations and lowers the risks of hallucinations. The experimental results demonstrate that the performance of the TLM_FQL is good with respect to metrics like cost, energy consumption, SLA violation, and scalability considering small and big fog computing scenarios. The results are further validated using expected value analysis and results are found to be satisfactory. For future work, trajectory will be used to properly represent the state, action, and reward of the learning agent. Pruning of unsafe trajectories will be carried out to enhance the robustness of the scheduling policies and improve learning efficiency. The noise in the state space is removed through selective experience replay and produces policy reliability under adverse operating conditions. The integration of safe reinforcement learning along with the trajectory pruning ensure faster learning and prevents the catastrophic failures of the tasks.

title: Generative AI-Enabled Transmission and Computing Integration for Cloud-Edge Collaborative IoT
abstract: Cloud–edge collaborative internet of things (IoT) enables consumer energy management by exploring task offloading and migration for low-latency processing. However, treating transmission and computing separately leads to inefficient resource utilization. Furthermore, in queue backlogs with long-tail distributions, traditional learning based approaches exhibit deteriorated performance because rare but critical backlog scenarios remain undervalued. Therefore, we propose a generative artificial intelligence (GAI)-enabled transmission and computing integration framework to intelligently distribute workloads based on channel conditions and cloud–edge capabilities. For transmission, GAI-assisted upper confidence bound (UCB) is proposed for device-side offloading compression ratio optimization. It adjusts exploration weight dynamically, ensuring robust transmission reliability under varying channel conditions. For computing, GAI-enhanced federated deep Q-network (FDQN) is developed to jointly optimize task migration and computing resource allocation. GAI synthesizes rare events and extreme experiences for augmented training of global DQN, enabling adaptive learning and robustness under extreme events. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm’s effectiveness in reducing delay while ensuring long-term transmission reliability. © 1975-2011 IEEE.

title: Generative AI-Enabled Transmission and Computing Integration for Cloud-Edge Collaborative IoT
---

## Generative AI-Enabled Transmission and Computing Integration for Cloud-Edge Collaborative IoT

Haoyu Ci, Yuxiang Leng, Ziming Li, Yiming Chen, Haijun Liao, Member, IEEE , Zhenyu Zhou, Senior Member, IEEE , Wenqing Wu, and Xiaoyan Wang, Senior Member, IEEE
Abstract -Cloud-edge collaborative internet of things (IoT) enables consumer energy management by exploring task offloading and migration for low-latency processing. However, treating transmission and computing separately leads to inefficient resource utilization. Furthermore, ∈ queue backlogs with longtail distributions, traditional learning based approaches exhibit deteriorated performance because rare but critical backlog scenarios remain undervalued. Therefore, we propose a generative artificial intelligence (GAI)-enabled transmission and computing integration framework to intelligently distribute workloads based on channel conditions and cloud-edge capabilities. For transmission, GAI-assisted upper confidence bound (UCB) is proposed for device-side offloading compression ratio optimization. It adjusts exploration weight dynamically, ensuring robust transmission reliability under varying channel conditions. For computing, GAI-enhanced federated deep Q-network (FDQN) is developed to jointly optimize task migration and computing resource allocation. GAI synthesizes rare events and extreme experiences for augmented training of global DQN, enabling adaptive learning and robustness under extreme events. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm's effectiveness ∈ reducing delay while ensuring long-term transmission reliability.

Index Terms -Cloud-edge collaboration, IoT, generative AI, upper confidence bound, transmission-computing integration.

## I. INTRODUCTION

∈ consumer-side energy management, internet of things (IoT) play a pivotal role ∈ real-time monitoring and optimal control. By embedding IoT nodes into distributed photovoltaics (PV), charging piles, and other electrical equipment, they continuously collect high-frequency electrical parameters [1], [2]. These data are uploaded to edge or cloud servers for processing and analysis [3], [4]. Edge servers, geographically close to consumers, can deliver millisecond-level responsiveness. However, their constrained computing and storage

H. Ci, Y. Leng, Z. Li, Y. Chen, H. Liao, Z. Zhou, and W. Wu are with the School of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, North China Electric Power University, Beijing 102206, China (e-mail: { haoyu ci, 120231020409, ziming li, yiming chen, haijun liao, zhenyu zhou, wenqing wu } @ncepu.edu.cn. Corresponding author: Haijun Liao .

X. Wang is with Graduate School of Science and Engineering, Ibaraki University, Ibaraki 316-8511, Japan (e-mail: xiaoyan.wang.shawn@vc.ibaraki.ac.jp).

capacities cause large queue backlogs under peak loads or traffic bursts [5]. Cloud servers, offer abundant computing resources but suffer from long-distance network congestion and transmission delay, exhibiting pronounced uncertainty [6], [7]. Therefore, it is imperative to construct a novel collaborative data processing framework combining the complemented advantages of cloud and edge for IoT-empowered consumer-side energy management. This new framework can dynamically balance load distributions from a global perspective and ensure low-latency high-reliability data processing.

Transmission and computing integration is the core for achieving cloud-edge collaborative IoT [8]. For transmission, by dynamically optimizing task-offloading compression ratio of IoT devices, the volume of end-edge offloaded data is substantially reduced [9]. For computing, fine-grained task migrations allow computation intensive tasks on heavilyloaded edge servers to be migrated ∈ real time to lightlyloaded edge or cloud servers, improving computing resource utilization efficiency and global load balance [10]. Moreover, computing resource allocation can be allocated on demand according to real-time queue backlogs, further suppressing queue oscillations [11]. However, transmission and computing integrated optimization is extremely complex considering the high dynamics of data arrivals, channel gains, and computing resource distributions. Conventional model-based approaches cannot be applied effectively due to the lack of global state information.

Reinforcement learning leverages a closed-loop interaction-feedback-update mechanism to perform distributed optimization without relying on exact mathematical models. Within this framework, upper confidence bound (UCB) which dynamically balances exploration and exploitation through a confidence-driven approach has been widely utilized ∈ IoT and end-edge transmission optimization. ∈ [12], Sun et al. developed a data offloading method leveraging UCB to cope with vehicular edge computing under dynamic mobility conditions. ∈ [13], Sun et al. proposed a task offloading method applying classical UCB ∈ vehicular cloud computing, which achieved minimized average offloading delay without real-time state exchange. ∈ [14], Qiao et al. introduced a trustworthy edge storage method based on UCB, which leveraged server trust values and data popularity to guide storage decisions. However, these methods generally focus on the decentralized arm-selection problem and are not suitable for high-dimensional optimization problems of transmission and computing integration. Its confidence bounds struggle to adapt to the dynamic interplay of large optimization dimensions, while its static exploration-exploitation trade-off fails to capture temporal correlations ∈ edge environments.

To tackle these challenges, deep Q-network (DQN) emerges as a promising alternative. It leverages deep reinforcement learning to approximate value functions for high-dimensional state-action spaces, enabling adaptive policy optimization [15]. ∈ [16], Hu et al. proposed a DQN-based task-migration method solving the dynamic offloading and resource allocation challenges under high-dimensional state-complex action coupling. ∈ [17], Cheng et al. presented a task offloading and resource allocation method that combines double DQN network update mechanism with dueling DQN dual-branch structure. ∈ [18], Zhou et al. proposed a method that applied the double DQN algorithm to joint computation offloading and resource allocation. Nevertheless, these approaches suffer from issues such as poor adaptations for extreme scenarios because of DQN's reliance on extensive real-world interactions for training [19]. They also make insufficient utilization of global observations due to the lack of collaborative model training and knowledge sharing.

Generative adversarial networks (GAN) as an important subset of generative artificial intelligence (GAI) leverage an adversarial training mechanism to accurately fit and extrapolate the probability distribution of real data ∈ the feature space, thereby providing deep reinforcement learning with highquality synthetic samples such as scarce channel trajectories and computing load distributions [20], [21]. This significantly expands the experience replay buffer and enhances the robustness of the policy network against extreme events. ∈ [22], Zhao et al. used a self-attention GAN to synthesize distributedgenerator outputs and line-damage data under extreme events and trained a double DQN on these samples. ∈ [23], Zhu et al. used Wasserstein GAN adversarial training mechanism to generate worst-case scenarios and iteratively injected them into DQN training, solving the issue of insufficient robustness. ∈ [24], Guo et al. introduced a GAN-powered routing scheme that generated network state samples ∈ real time and integrated them into DQN for routing decisions.

Despite recent research progresses, many critical issues remain open. First, although recent literature actively addresses joint communication and data compression ∈ IoT edge networks, existing studies generally treat these transmission strategies and computing resource allocation as completely independent problems. This lack of an integrated framework inevitably leads to heterogeneous resource mismatch and suboptimal optimization performances. Second, traditional UCB-based approaches cannot dynamically adjust exploration weights based on channel conditions. This results ∈ insufficient exploration during good channel states or excessive packet loss during poor channel conditions, violating the longterm transmission reliability constraint. Last but not least, rare events such as severe traffic bursts and drastic channel deteriorations induce long-tail distributions ∈ system states [25]. Consequently, the joint optimization of edge-side task migration and computing resource allocation suffers from sample scarcity. This causes severe performance degradation under extreme events despite GAN-assisted DQN training. These shortcomings stem from the lack of conditional control over the generation process, making it challenging to generate specific or targeted data samples required ∈ complex transmission-computing integration scenarios.

To address the above challenges, we propose a GAIenabled transmission and computing integration algorithm for cloud-edge collaborative IoT. First, we formulate a lowlatency task data processing problem for consumer energy management applications. The objective is to minimize the long-term average end-to-end cloud-edge collaborative processing delay. Second, leveraging Lyapunov optimization, the long-term latency minimization and reliability constraints are decoupled into per-slot deterministic optimization subproblems, i.e., task offloading compression ratio decisions subproblem, and task migration and edge computing resource allocation subproblem. For transmission optimization, the proposed algorithm leverages GAN-assisted UCB to solve the first subproblem. For computing optimization, the proposed algorithm leverages GAI-enhanced FDQN to solve the second subproblem. The main contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:

- 1. GAI-enabled transmission and computing integration framework for cloud-edge collaborative IoT: By unifying data transmission and computing optimization, this novel framework intelligently distributes workloads based on real-time channel conditions and cloud-edge capabilities. It facilitates adaptive resource sharing, parallel processing, and GAI-assisted learning enhancement to improve load balancing and minimize data processing delay.
- 2. GAI-assisted UCB for device-side offloading compression ratio optimization: It leverages GAN to extrapolate channel gain distributions for dynamic exploration weight adjustment. Exploration is prioritized to discover optimal strategies during high-quality channel states, while exploitation of known reliable configurations is emphasized under poor channel conditions. This intelligent adaptation to channel gain fluctuations ensures robust transmission reliability.
- 3. GAI-enhanced FDQN for joint edge task migration and computing-resource optimization: It leverages conditional GAN (CGAN) to synthesize rare events and extreme experiences for augmented training of global DQN. The enhanced global model is distributed to edge servers through a performance-aware weighted federated aggregation mechanism. This approach effectively mitigates challenges posed by scarce long-tail samples and unobservable global states. It ensures adaptive learning across heterogeneous environments and significantly enhancing policy's robustness to extreme events.

## II. SYSTEM MODEL

The proposed framework of transmission and computing integration for cloud-edge collaborative IoT is shown ∈ Fig. 1. It consists of device layer, edge layer, cloud layer, and application layer.

Fig. 1. The proposed integrated framework for cloud-edge collaborative IoT transmission and computing.

- Application Layer : Based on the data processed by edge and cloud layers, this layer supports consumer energy management applications, including distributed PV regulation, consumer electric vehicle (EV) charge and discharge management, consumer home energy management, and consumer demand response.
- Cloud Layer : The cloud layer contains high-performance cloud server with powerful storage and computing capabilities. Through cloud-edge collaboration, cloud servers process data migrated from edge servers. FDQN architecture is adopted to train the global model via federated aggregation. ∈ addition, two GANs are deployed to generate channel state samples for IoT devices and queue backlog distribution samples for edge servers, guiding integrated optimization of transmission and computation.
- Edge Layer : Multiple edge servers are deployed to process data for IoT devices within their coverage areas. An edge server with heavy queue backlog can migrate

its tasks to lightly loaded edge servers or the cloud, improving load balancing and reducing queuing delay. Each edge server independently trains its integrated optimization model based on the global model distributed by the cloud, enabling joint optimization of task migration and edge computing resource allocation.

- Device Layer : The device layer includes numerous IoT devices deployed on various power grid facilities, such as distributed PVs, electric vehicles, charging piles, consumer loads, and energy storage batteries. These devices collect real-time operating data and key state parameters. IoT devices compress the collected data and offload them to edge servers for processing.

Edge servers are constrained by limited resources and wireless channel fluctuations. Cloud-edge data migration experiences high variability and uncertainty due to routing, congestion, and long-distance communication, which have become a critical bottleneck. Therefore, cloud-edge collaboration has become essential. By processing delay-sensitive tasks at edge and migrating overloaded tasks to the cloud, a balance between computing efficiency and response delay is achieved.

A slotted model with slot length τ is considered, and the slot set is defined as T = { 1 , . . . , t, . . . , T } . For transmission, IoT devices optimize the compression ratio for task offloading. For computing, edge servers optimize task migration decisions and computing resource allocation. The goal is to minimize the end-to-end cloud-edge collaborative processing delay, subject to transmission reliability and resource allocation constraints. To provide a quick reference, the principal notations used ∈ this paper are summarized ∈ Table I.

## III. PROBLEM FORMULATION AND TRANSFORMATION

This paper aims to address the low-latency task data processing problem for consumer energy management applications. The goal is to minimize the long-term average end-toend cloud-edge collaborative processing delay through integrated transmission and computation. Specifically, for transmission, devices optimize task offloading compression ratio decisions. For computing, edge servers jointly optimize task migration and edge computing resource allocation based on the global model distributed by the cloud. The problem is formulated as

Here, y = { y k ( t ) } denotes the set of task offloading compression ratio strategy, x = { x k,j,j ′ ( t ) } denotes the set of task migration decisions, and f = { f j ( t ) } denotes the set of computing resource allocation policies. C 1 ensures that each task is processed by one edge server or the cloud server. C 2 represents the edge server's available computation resource constraint, where f max j ( t ) is the maximum available computation resource for edge server j . C 3 represents the long-term transmission reliability constraint, where L max k,j is the pre-set threshold for the average packet loss over time. Specifically, this long-term constraint ensures the statistical completeness of data, providing critical support for realtime monitoring and optimal control ∈ consumer-side energy management. Furthermore, this statistical guarantee acts as a soft constraint that strategically tolerates short-term reliability violations, thereby significantly improving resource utilization efficiency.

## IV. GAI-ENABLED TRANSMISSION AND COMPUTING INTEGRATION ALGORITHM FOR CLOUD-EDGE COLLABORATIVE IOT

∈ this section, we present the proposed GAI-enabled transmission and computing integration algorithm for cloud-edge collaborative IoT. The framework of the algorithm is illustrated ∈ Fig. 2. First, it employs GAI-assisted UCB algorithm to solve SP1 . Second, it employs GAI-enhanced FDQN algorithm to solve SP2 .

## A. GAI-assisted UCB for Device-side Offloading Compression Ratio Optimization

Traditional UCB lacks adaptability to external dynamics. ∈ scenarios with channel quality fluctuations, UCB relying only on historical statistics fails to achieve optimal responsiveness. To address this issue, we propose a GAI-assisted UCB algorithm for optimizing device-side task offloading compression ratio strategies. By integrating GAN-based channel state generation, the proposed algorithm enhances decision foresight. The proposed GAN structure consists of a generator and a discriminator. The generator generates channel states based on historical channel gains and noise/interference information. The discriminator distinguishes between real channel states and the generator's generated states. During training, the generator optimizes to deceive the discriminator, while the discriminator improves its recognition capability. Through adversarial training, both networks are iteratively improved until the generator outputs channel states close to the real one.

The GAN generator takes the historical channel state sequence ˜ H k,j as input and produces a predicted future channel sequence H k,j = { h k,j (1) , h k,j (2) , . . . , h k,j ( T ) } . The adversarial training objective is defined as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Here, G denotes the generator and D denotes the discriminator. During training, D learns to distinguish real channel states from generated ones, while G is improved under the feedback from D to approximate the true distribution. This iterative optimization enables the model to capture both statistical and temporal features of the channel, improving generation accuracy. The operation processes are as follow.

The proposed GAI-enhanced FDQN framework is illustrated ∈ Fig. 2. It performs joint training through cloud-edge federated aggregation, which improves convergence efficiency and global generalization. A dual-mode adaptive mechanism controls model uploading, balancing global performance improvement and communication overhead. The cloud deploys a CGAN to address data scarcity ∈ extreme cases for federated learning. The conditional variable c describes macro network conditions, with smaller values indicating worse scenarios. CGAN generates extreme experiences online, which are used to strengthen the aggregated global model before it is distributed to edge servers. Each edge server deploys a DQN agent with a main network θ main j ( t ) and a target network θ target j ( t ) of the same structure. The main network estimates Q-values for real-time decisions, while the target network provides stable targets for training. Historical experiences are replayed to reduce data correlation and improve learning optimality. Within this integrated architecture, the local agent observes its current state and selects an action, which includes task migration and edge computing resource allocation, while the cloud aggregates uploaded models by federated averaging with performance weights. The execution process is shown ∈ Algorithm 1. The details are as follows:

- 1. Edge Server Initialization: Each edge server initializes all real physical queues and virtual queues with backlog and indicator variables set to zero.
- 2. Model Downloading: At the beginning of each time slot, if ( t -1) mod D = 0 or a performance alert is triggered ∈ the previous slot, the edge server downloads the global model and sets θ main j ( t ) = θ main G,en ( t ) and θ target j ( t ) = θ target G,en ( t ) .
- 3. Action Selection and Execution at Edge Server: Each edge server observes its current state S j ( t ) . Then, based on the main network θ main j ( t ) , it computes the Q-value for each action Q ( S j ( t ) , A j ( t ) , θ main j ( t )) . ## V. SIMULATION

We evaluate the efficacy of the proposed algorithm ∈ MATLAB. The simulated scenario encompasses a 2 km × 3 km area subdivided into six sub-regions, each hosting one edge server for 50 IoT devices. Two services are considered: (i) distribution automation, which imposes stringent delay constraints but tolerates moderate packet loss. (ii) electricity information collection, which prioritizes ultra-reliable delivery with relaxed delay demands. Devices access to their respective edge servers via 5G links configured with 0.2 MHz bandwidth and 50 mW transmit power. The interference is characterized by an alpha-stable distribution [29]. Detailed simulation parameters are provided ∈ Table II [30]-[32]. These parameter settings are rigorously configured according to established 5G standards and commercial edge hardware specifications to realistically reflect the physical constraints of consumer energy management networks, thereby guaranteeing the validity and reproducibility of the simulated results.

We utilize two comparison algorithms. Baseline 1 is a UCB and distributed DQN enabled cloud-edge collaborative algorithm. Each device applies UCB for compression ratio selection, while each edge server uses DQN for migration and resource allocation [33]. Baseline 2 is a federated DQN based cloud-edge collaborative optimization algorithm. Each edge server runs a DQN agent for joint optimization of compression, migration, and resource allocation, with local models periodically aggregated ∈ the cloud [34]. Both baselines reformulate the long-term reliability constraint via a penalty term and minimize the weighted ∑ of end-to-end delay and reliability penalty. They are explicitly selected to highlight the deficiencies relative to the proposed algorithm. Baseline 1 lacks the integrated coordination of transmission and computing. Baseline 2 lacks GAI enhancement, rendering it vulnerable to extreme scenarios with long-tail distributions.

Figure 3 shows the end-to-end delay and average packet loss among different algorithms. Compared with Baseline 1, the proposed algorithm reduces end-to-end delay by 29.65%, while the average packet loss also decreases by 0.68%. Baseline 2 achieves better delay performance at the cost of failing to satisfy the packet loss constraint. ∈ contrast, the proposed algorithm can stably achieve a balanced trade-off between end-to-end delay and packet loss. This improvement comes from the GAI-assisted UCB, which generates channel states through GAN to enhance exploration efficiency, thereby improving convergence speed and optimality. ∈ addition, the GAI-enhanced FDQN adopts a dual-mode model uploading strategy, while the cloud aggregates models based on packet loss performance, suppressing abnormal updates and amplifying high-quality models. Moreover, CGAN generates extreme scenario data to enhance global model robustness.

Figure 4 shows the end-to-end delay and compression ratio selection ∈ channel degradation scenarios. When the channel condition is normal, the proposed algorithm reduces the endto-end delay of Service 2 by 54.91% and that of Service 1 by 17.47% compared with UCB. This is because the proposed algorithm can flexibly select compression ratios according to service requirements: for delay-sensitive Service 1, it prefers lower compression ratios to reduce delay, while for packetloss-sensitive Service 2, it selects higher compression ratios to reduce packet loss. ∈ contrast, traditional UCB fails to differentiate between services. After the channel deteriorates at the 100-th slot, the proposed algorithm quickly adapts by adjusting exploration weights with GAI assistance, achieving a rapid decrease ∈ delay to a new optimum while maintaining service differentiation. Traditional UCB, however, relies solely on historical experience, leading to a surge ∈ delay and severe performance degradation.

Figure 5 shows average end-to-end delay with respect to the number of devices. As the number of devices per edge server increases, the network experiences heavier load, increasing end-to-end delays. When the number of devices reaches 360, the proposed algorithm achieves 45.56% lower delay than baseline1, respectively. GAI-enhanced FDQN dynamically adjusts task migration to minimize end-to-end delay. Meanwhile, GAI-assisted UCB effectively identifies channel congestion and adaptively adjusts compression ratios, ensuring low delay under heavy-load conditions.

Figure 6 illustrates the training process of the proposed algorithm and Baseline 2 under federated DQN. The proposed algorithm achieves stable convergence and reduces the normalized loss function by 49.33% at the 200 -th slot compared with Baseline 2. This is attributed to federated averaging, allocating model weights based on performance to suppress poor updates and enhance global stability. Moreover, by prioritizing high-quality experiences during early training, the algorithm reduces ineffective exploration and further accelerates convergence.
